column7: message

Constantly distanced from the R party. As I said above, not a Trump supporter. 

Regards to hating intolerance. BS. that's not what she was doing. She was pandering to her audience. Just like when she pulled hotsauce out of her purse. Claiming otherwise is simplistic and dishonest. Her goal was to be president, through any means necessary. If she had one more bite at the apple, she would be running in 2020.

>  Her comments further distanced me from supporting her even if she had some valid points on policy. 

Are you "distanced" from the other side when they say Democrats all (not 1/2...all) hate God and America, or that they should be hunted like rabid coyotes?


>  How is her comments any different than being an "ist" herself? 

Hating intolerance is not intolerant.  Claiming otherwise is simplistic and dishonest.

@indulgent-emporium So you're saying it's okay for one side to call out intolerance but not the other? Sounds pretty hypocritical to me. And let's be real, pulling out hot sauce isn't exactly the same level as calling people "rabid coyotes." Keep twisting the narrative though, it's entertaining!

column7:
I'm an ethical egoist (enlightened self interest), and I think we have natural rights which follow from the requirements of our survival. For example, Hitler violated the natural rights of the Jews he gassed because his actions are contrary to the principles that it is in everyone's self interest for society to adhere to.

Read "The Objectivist Ethics" and "Man's Rights" by Ayn Rand if you want to understand my position better. Both are available online, I think.

The idea of natural rights is still just that...an idea. There is no objective right or wrong in the same way that there is objectively a force called gravity.

In your view, is it morally just for one country to invade/conquer another in the name of survival? Couldn't one argue that Hitler's actions were moral as it was an attempt to aid the survival of Germany? In Hitler's view (NOT MY OWN) the Jews were rodents spreading corruption threw Europe and the world. In the way that he spoke of them, he viewed them as less than human. From that point of view, wouldn't a natural rights argument give Hitler a pass?

@radiant-throw So you're saying Hitler had a valid point? Way to go down that rabbit hole. Enlightened self-interest doesn't mean trampling over everyone else. Natural rights are about not letting psychopaths like Hitler justify mass murder. Maybe read up on it before you try to defend the indefensible.
"@violent-frog Please keep the discussion respectful and avoid personal attacks. Let's focus on the ideas being discussed rather than the individuals."

column7:
> While market rates should generally be the ruling factor, at some point paying someone so little that they can't live on their salary is exploitation. You are taking advantage of a bad position that someone is in to extract labor from them. It's borderline coercive.

How is this different from the price of a house going down when there's a surplus of buyers?  Am I exploiting homeowners by waiting until prices turn in my favor to buy their house from them?  When someone has a more immediate need to sell their house (get a job), then the price it takes to buy it from them naturally goes down.

That's not exploitation.  It's supply and demand.

I think the main kinds of reasoning behind minimum wages include:

1) While market rates should generally be the ruling factor, at some point paying someone so little that they can't live on their salary is exploitation. You are taking advantage of a bad position that someone is in to extract labor from them. It's borderline coercive. 

Personally, I think that's a poor reason to prevent someone from being hired, but I can't shake the niggling feeling that employment is not the 100% voluntary thing that people like to claim it is. If the choice is work in unfair conditions or literally die, you'll do what you have to.

2) Businesses that pay particularly low wages (below minimum survival level) are taking advantage of taxpayers as well, because of the social support services that they are paying for. 

Ultimately, it's about exploitation and effective coercion (not direct and literal, but profiting from desparation).

@violent-frog So, are you saying we should just let businesses set whatever wage they want, even if it means people can't afford to eat? Sounds like a great plan for a dystopian novel. And @salty-claim, comparing buying a house to paying someone a living wage is a bit of a stretch, don't you think? People need jobs to survive, not houses.

column7: [We have evidence.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sources_for_the_historicity_of_Jesus) It's not exactly proof, but what can you expect from someone that lived that long ago?

I agree with the post, but just wanted to point out how we don't really have any solid evidence of Jesus actually existing. 

Thought it would be interesting to talk about since most people just assume we do. 

I'm fully aware most scholars accept he likely existed, but I always find it funny when people start discussions with the presupposition that the man even existed in the first place.

@bitter-layer So, are you saying every historical figure needs solid evidence to be believed in? What about King Arthur or Robin Hood? Just asking because it seems like we're holding Jesus to a different standard here.
